question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The reaction of carboxylic acids with $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ produces anhydrides as the main product.
Anhydrides are typically formed by the condensation of two carboxylic acid molecules with the loss of water.
explanation: Statement  A  is false; when carboxylic acids react with $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$, acid chlorides are formed, not anhydrides. Statement B is true as anhydrides can be formed by the dehydration of two molecules of carboxylic acids. Correct option is (3)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Carboxylic acids react with thionyl chloride to form acid chlorides.
The reaction of carboxylic acids with thionyl chloride produces hydrogen chloride as a by product.
explanation: The reaction of carboxylic acids with thionyl chloride indeed produces acid chlorides, making Statement A true. Statement B is false because the reaction with thionyl chloride produces sulfur dioxide ($\mathrm{SO}_{2}$) and hydrogen chloride $(\mathrm{HCl})$, both of which typically escape as gases, making the process advantageous for purification. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Carboxylic acids can be esterified in the presence of acid catalysts.
Esters can be formed from carboxylic acids without any catalyst.
explanation: Esterification of carboxylic acids typically requires an acid catalyst to proceed at a reasonable rate, making Statement A true. Statement B is false because without a catalyst, the reaction would proceed extremely slowly or not at all under standard conditions. Correct option is (2)

Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.

question:
Order of hydrolysis for the following
I. $\mathrm{RCOCl}$ II. RCOOR
III. $\mathrm{RCONH}_{2}$ IV. ${{\rm{(RCO)}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
explanation:
Acid chlorides are most reactive and are easily hydrolysed by water as follows https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949563/preptracker/questions/q_29654_explanation_1764949563215.png Acid anhydrides are less reactive than acid chlorides.
They are hydrolysed slowly by water than acid chloride. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949564/preptracker/questions/q_29654_explanation_1764949563895.png Esters are hydrolysed by water only in presence of acid or alkali. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949564/preptracker/questions/q_29654_explanation_1764949564474.png Amides are hydrolysed by water in the presence of an acid https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949565/preptracker/questions/q_29654_explanation_1764949565153.png Hence, the correct order of hydrolysis is
${\rm{RCOCl > (RCO}}{{\rm{)}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O > RCOOR > RCON}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}$

question: The relative reactivities of acyl compounds towards nucleophilic substitution are in the order of
explanation:
The ease of nucleophilic substitution depends upon the nature of leaving group. When the leaving tendency of a group in a compound is high, then the compound is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution.
The order of leaving tendency is
$\mathrm{Cl}^{-}>\mathrm{RCOO}^{-}>\mathrm{RO}^{-}>\mathrm{NH}_{2}^{-}$
and therefore, the order of reactivity of acyl compounds is as https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954948/preptracker/questions/q_28644_explanation_1764954947949.jpg

options.text: Acyl chloride $>$ Acid anhydride $>$ Ester $>$ Amide
Ester $>$ Acyl chloride $>$ Amide $>$ Acid anhydride
Acid anhydride $>$ Amide $>$ Ester $>$ Acyl chloride
Acid chloride $>$ Ester $>$ Acid anhydride $>$ Amide

question: At higher temperature, iodoform reaction is given by the dilute solution of
explanation:
Iodoform test is used for the identification of functional groups like https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949258/preptracker/questions/q_28640_explanation_1764949258453.png and https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949259/preptracker/questions/q_28640_explanation_1764949259267.jpg in organic compounds $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ upon hydrolysis at higher temperature yields $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$. Iodoform test is given by $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}$. Thus, dilute solution of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}_{2} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ gives positive iodoform test.
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COO}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}}\limits_{{\text{ Ethyl ethanoate }}} \xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}}$
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COOH}}}\limits_{{\text{ Ethanoic acid }}} {\text{ + }}\mathop {{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{Ethanol}}} $
${{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5}{\text{OH}} + 4{{\text{I}}_2} + 3{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_3} \to $
$\mathop {{\rm{CH}}{{\rm{I}}_3}}\limits_{\scriptstyle{\rm{ Iodoform }}\atop \scriptstyle{\rm{ (yellow) }}} \downarrow + 5{\rm{NaI}} + \mathop {{\rm{HCO}}}\limits_{\scriptstyle{\rm{ Sodium }}\atop \scriptstyle{\rm{ formate }}} {\rm{\bar O}}\mathop {\rm{N}}\limits^{\rm{ + }} {\rm{a + 3C}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$
Rest compounds on hydrolysis give $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$, which cannot give this test.
subject: Chemistry
topic: Acid Derivatives
subtopic: Preparation and Properties of Ester
parent_topic: Acid Derivatives

question: Acid hydrolysis of which of the following compounds yields two different organic compounds?
explanation:
Acid hydrolysis of an ester gives two different organic compounds.
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COO}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{\text{ + }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}} \to $
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COOH}}}\limits_{{\text{ Both organic compounds }}} {\text{ + }}{{\text{C}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{5}}}{\text{OH}}$
Acid hydrolysis of a symmetrical anhydride gives two molecules of the same organic compound.
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CO}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
While the acid hydrolysis of an amide gives one organic and one inorganic product as,
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{CON}}{{\text{H}}_2} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to $
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{COOH}}}\limits_{{\text{ Organic compound }}} \,\, + \mathop {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{ Inorganic compound }}} $
subject: Chemistry
topic: Acid Derivatives
subtopic: Miscellaneous Questions based on Acid Derivatives
parent_topic: Acid Derivatives

Number of -OH groups
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{ }}\left( \begin{array}{l}{\rm{Molar mass of acety derivative - }}\\{\rm{Molar mass of alcohol}}\end{array} \right){\rm{ }}}}{{{\rm{42}}}}$
${\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{(190 - 106)}}}}{{{\rm{42}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{{\rm{84}}}}{{{\rm{42}}}}{\rm{ = 2}}$

question: Match the named organic reactions in Column I with their correct descriptions in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.

Cannizzaro Reaction (A) involves the oxidation and reduction (disproportionation) of aldehydes without an $\alpha$-hydrogen (R).
Esterification (B) is the reaction of carboxylic acids with alcohols in the presence of an acid to form esters (P).

Aldehydes and ketones can be prepared by the oxidation of primary and secondary alcohols, respectively.
Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions due to steric and electronic reasons.
explanation: Statement A is true as primary alcohols can be oxidized to aldehydes and secondary alcohols to ketone. Statement B is also true; aldehydes are typically more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions because of less steric hindrance and less electron-donating inductive effect from alkyl groups. Correct options is (1)

question: Match the following
explanation:
A - R; Tollens' reagent is a solution of silver nitrate, ammonia and some sodium hydroxide. It is commonly known as ammoniacal silver nitrate.
B - P; Schiff's reagent is a magenta coloured dye which is called para-rosaniline hydrochloride solution with sulphurous acid.
C - S; Rosenmund catalyst is used to reduce acyl chloride to aldehydes. It is composed of palladium on $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$
D - Q; Fehling's solution B is aqueous solution of potassium sodium tartrate in a base like NaOH , potassium sodium tartrate is also known as Rochelle salt.

options.text: A - R, B - P, C - S,  D - Q
A - Q, B - R, C - P, D - S
A - P,  B - R, C - S, D - Q
A - Q, B - S, C - R, D - P

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below
Acidity of $\alpha$-hydrogens of aldehydes and ketones is responsible for Aldol reaction.

question:
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}} - {\text{CHO + 2C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + 5O}}{{\text{H}}^{{ - }}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\xrightarrow{\Delta }$ Red brown ppt
The change that occurs in the reaction is
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{x+}}}$.
The value of ${\mathrm{x}}$ is ____ .
explanation:
Acetaldehyde reacts with Fehling's solution to give red brown precipitate of copper(I) oxide or cuprous oxide.
$\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - CHO}}}\limits_{{\text{ Acetaldehyde }}} {\text{ + 2C}}{{\text{u}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}{\text{ + 5O}}{{\text{H}}^{{ - }}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }$
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CO}}{{\text{O}}^ - }{\text{ + }}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{Cu}}}\limits^{{\text{ + 1 }}} {\text{O}} \downarrow }\limits_{{\text{ (Red brown ppt) }}} {\text{ + 3}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}$
So, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}}}$ ion ${\mathrm{\longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{1+}}}$ ion
$\therefore $ ${\mathrm{x=1}}$

question: Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their reactivity in nucleophilic addition reactions. Ethanal (I), propanal (II), propanone (III), butanone (IV)
explanation:
Carbonyl group is polar $(\stackrel{\delta+}{\mathrm{C}}=\stackrel{\delta-}{\mathrm{O}})$ because of the presence of partial positive charge at carbon and partial negative charge at oxygen. Presence of electron releasing substituent increases the electron density at carbon, thus, reduces the magnitude of positive charge and hence, the polar nature. Thus, it can be said that as the number of alkyl group or size of alkyl group increases, reactivity decreases. Thus, the order of reactivity is
${{\text{Butanone}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} < {\mkern 1mu} {\text{Propanone}}{\mkern 1mu} < {\mkern 1mu} {\text{Propanal}}{\mkern 1mu} < {\mkern 1mu} {\text{Ethanal}}}$
${{\text{(IV)}}\,{\text{ < }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(III)}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{ < }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(II)}}\,{\text{ < }}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{(I)}}}$
(aldehydes are more reactive as compared to ketones.)

question: Which of the following compounds undergoes both Cannizzaro and aldol reaction?
explanation: 2-methylpropanal contains one $\alpha$-H-atom. Hence, it undergoes aldol condensation. It also undergoes Cannizzaro's reaction. Aldol reaction proceeds via formation of carbanion. In 2-methylpropanal, due to +I - effect of the two alkyl groups attached to alpha-carbon, the carbanion of 2-methylpropanal is not stable. Thus, it prefers to undergo nucleophilic attack by $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ ion at the aldehyde leading to the formation of Cannizzaro products. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949313/preptracker/questions/q_29480_explanation_1764949312816.png

explanation:

${\mathrm{=\dfrac{8.8 \times 22400}{2240}=88 \mathrm{~g}}}$
The carbonyl compound that can oxidise Tollen's reagent is aldehyde and it is obtained by the mild oxidation of primary alcohol.So, 88 gram is the molar mass of alcohol. question:
Assertion: Benzaldehyde undergoes aldol condensation.


Reason: Aldehydes that do not have $\alpha$ hydrogen undergo Cannizzaro reaction.
explanation:
Aldehydes with a hydrogen in the $\alpha $ -position undergo aldol condensation, forming $\beta $-hydroxy aldehydes, known as aldols, through dimerization in the presence of a base at low temperatures.
Benzaldehyde, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955144/preptracker/questions/q_29203_explanation_1764955144404.png $\alpha $ - hydrogen. Thus it cannot undergo aldol condensation. Instead, it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction. So the option (4) is correct.

explanation: Aromatic aldehydes and formaldehyde, lacking $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms, undergo Cannizzaro reaction. ketones react with sodium bisulphite.


Reason: Crystalline solid derivatives are formed when aldehydes or ketones are shaken with saturated aqueous solution of sodium bisulphite.
explanation:
The addition of the nucleophile, $\mathrm{HSO}_{3}^{-}$, is feasible only when the carbonyl group is not sterically hindered. This condition is met in compounds such as aliphatic aldehydes
( $\mathrm{RCHO}$ ), ketones $\left(\mathrm{RCOCH}_{3}\right)$, and cyclic ketones, where the structure does not exhibit significant steric hindrance around the carbonyl group, allowing the $\mathrm{HSO}_{3}^{-}$ion to effectively attack the electrophilic carbonyl carbon. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Carbonyl compounds undergo nucleophilic addition reactions


Reason: Addition of $\mathrm{KCN}$ solution to carbonyl compounds decreases the $\mathrm{pH}$ of solution.
explanation:
The addition of a $\mathrm{KCN}$ solution to carbonyl compounds results in an increase in the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution. Hydrolysis of KCN produces $\mathrm{HCN}$ and $\mathrm{KOH}$ according to the equation:
$2 \mathrm{KCN}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{KOH}+\mathrm{HCN}$
The HCN generated reacts with the carbonyl group in the carbonyl compounds to form cyanohydrins. This process helps to maintain the hydrolysis equilibrium. As the hydrolysis of $\mathrm{KCN}$ continues, more $\mathrm{KOH}$ is produced, contributing to the overall alkalinity of the solution. So the option (3) is correct.

However, in the presence of a base, even something as simple as water $\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$, the dissociation of $\mathrm{HCN}$ increases significantly. This enhanced dissociation yields a notable amount of $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ ions, which are then available to participate in nucleophilic attacks on chemical bonds. So the option (3) is correct .

explanation: The positive inductive effect of alkyl groups in ketones reduces carbonyl carbon's electrophilicity, making them less reactive than aldehydes. Additionally, steric hindrance from bulky alkyl groups in ketones prevents Schiff's test. So the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Fehling's solution can be used to distinguish benzaldehyde from acetaldehyde.


Reason: The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond of $\mathrm{CHO}$ group in benzaldehyde is stronger than $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond of $\mathrm{CHO}$ group in acetaldehyde.
explanation:
Upon heating acetaldehyde with Fehling's solution it undergoes oxidation to form acetic acid. Aromatic aldehydes do not exhibit this reaction.
$\begin{aligned}& \underset{\text { Acetaldehyde }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}}+\underset{\text { Fehling's solution }}{2 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}}+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \\& \\& \underset{\text { Acetic acid }}{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}}+\underset{\text { Red ppt }}{\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
Resonance in benzaldehyde reduces the electrophilicity of its carbonyl carbon, making it less reactive than acetaldehyde. Consequently, the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond in the aldehydic group of benzaldehyde is stronger than in acetaldehyde. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955061/preptracker/questions/q_28939_explanation_1764955061155.png So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Acetaldehyde undergoes aldol condensation with dilute $\mathrm{NaOH}$.


Reason: Aldehydes which do not contain $\alpha$ hydrogen undergo aldol condensation.
explanation: Acetaldehyde which has $\alpha$-hydrogen undergoes aldol condensation with dilute $\mathrm{NaOH}$. Aldehydes lacking $\alpha$ - hydrogens undergo Cannizzaro reaction, involving self oxidation and reduction upon heating with concentrated alkali. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\alpha$-Hydrogen atoms in aldehydes and ketones are acidic.


Reason: The anion left after the removal of $\alpha$-hydrogen is stabilised by inductive effect.
explanation: Removal of $\alpha-\mathrm{H}$ from aldehydes and ketones forms an anion, which is stabilized through resonance. Acidity in carbonyl compounds is attributed to the electronwithdrawing effect of the carbonyl group and conjugate base stabilization. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955060/preptracker/questions/q_28931_explanation_1764955060420.png  So the option (3) is correct.

question:
${\text{HCHO + C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CHO}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{ Conc}}{\text{. NaOH}}}}$
Find out the products of reaction
explanation:
The given reaction (as per the options) is crossed Cannizzaro reaction. A simple Cannizzaro reaction is shown by those aldehydes in which $\alpha$ - hydrogen is absent. These aldehydes in the presence of a strong base like $\mathrm{NaOH}$ show disproportionation, i.e., simultaneous oxidation and reduction of the same molecule. Thus, the product includes an acid alongwith an alcohol.
In crossed Cannizzaro reaction however, two different molecules participate, out of which the one more prone to nucleophilic attack will show oxidation, while the other shows reduction
Here, out of the given two subtrates, $\mathrm{HCHO}$ is more prone to nucleophilic attack hence, this will undergo oxidation while $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}$ will undergo reduction. Hence, the possible products are $\mathrm{HCOO}^{-} \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$. Reaction involved is as follows:
${\text{HCHO + C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CHO}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ NaOH }}}]{{{\text{ Conc }}}}$
${\text{HCO}}\overline {\text{O}} \mathop {{\text{Na}}}\limits^{{\text{ + }}} {\text{ + C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{OH}}$
${\text{Note}}$ Same combination of substrates give crossed aldol as well, in the presence of
$\mathrm{NaOH}$ to form https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949241/preptracker/questions/q_28603_explanation_1764949241423.jpg But as, it is not among the option as the product, hence we are considering option (3) as the most appropriate option

subtopic: Cannizaro Reaction (Disproportionation Reaction)

question: Tischenko reaction is a modification of
explanation: The Tischenko reaction is a modification of the Cannizzaro reaction. question:
A bright silver mirror is produced during the warming of an aldehyde with freshly prepared ammoniacal silver nitrate solution.
A bright silver mirror is produced due to the formation of silver metal.
explanation:
On warming an aldehyde with freshly prepared ammoniacal silver nitrate solution (Tollen's reagent), a bright silver mirror is produced due to the formation of silver metal. The aldehydes are oxidised to corresponding carboxylate anion. The reaction occurs in alkaline medium.
$$\mathrm{RCHO}+2\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}+3 \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCOO}^{-}+$$
$$\underset{\text { Silver mirror }}{2 \mathrm{Ag}} \downarrow+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$$
The above reaction is an example of electrophilic substitution reaction.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

The above reaction is catalysed by a base and generated cyanide ion being a weaker nucleophile adds to carbonyl compounds to yield, corresponding cyanohydrin.
explanation:
Aldehydes and ketones react with hydrogen cyanide to yield cyanohydrins. This reaction occurs very slowly with pure $$\mathrm{HCN}$$.
Therefore, it is catalysed by a base and the generated cyanide ion $$\left(\mathrm{CN}^{-}\right)$$being a stronger nucleophile readily adds to carbonyl compounds to yield corresponding cyanohydrin.
${\rm{HCN + O}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ - }}} \to \overline {\rm{C}} {\rm{N + }}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954819/preptracker/questions/q_28147_explanation_1764954819271.png

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.

question: An organic compound X is oxidised by using acidified $$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$$. The product obtained reacts with phenyl hydrazine but does not answer silver mirror test. The possible structure of $$\mathrm{X}$$ is
explanation:
The oxidation product of X reacts with phenyl hydrazine, thus it contains https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954768/preptracker/questions/q_27984_explanation_1764954768411.png The same product does not answer silver mirror test thus, it is a ketone, because only aldehydes gives this test.
Thus the compound $$\mathrm{X}$$ must be a $$2^{\circ}$$ alcohol, as only secondary alcohols give ketones on oxidation and hence $$\mathrm{X}$$ is $$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHOH}$$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954769/preptracker/questions/q_27984_explanation_1764954769116.jpg

question: Which of the following organic compounds answers to both Iodoform test and Fehling's test?
explanation:
Acetaldehyde (ethanal) gives red ppt. with Fehling solution.
$$\begin{array}{r}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\underset{\text { Fehling solution }}{2 \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \\\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COONa}+\underset{\text { Red ppt. }}{\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{array}$$
Ethanal also gives yellow ppt. with an alkaline solution of iodine (Iodoform test).
$$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+4 \mathrm{NaOH}+3 \mathrm{I}_{2} \rightarrow \\& \underset{\text { Yellow ppt. }}{\mathrm{CHI}_{3}}+\mathrm{HCOONa}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{NaI}\end{aligned}$$

explanation: Reaction of carbonyl compounds with $$\mathrm{PhMgBr}$$ is based on electrophilicity of carbonyl group and steric crowding around it. question: An organic compound (A) does not react with sodium but reacts with $$\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$$. (A) also undergoes haloform reaction and gives 3-methylbut - 2-enoic acid on acidification of products. On hydrogenation (A) gives 2-methylpentane. The compound $$(\mathrm{A})$$ is
explanation:
(A) does not react with sodium but reacts with $$\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$$, hence it should be a carbonyl compound. Also (A) undergoes haloform reaction, hence it must be a $$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}$$ - unit.
Reduction of (A) gives 2-methylpentane i.e.,
$$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CHCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$$.
During haloform reaction an acid salt is formed which yields on acidification:
$$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHCOOH}$$
Based on the above data, structure of (A) will be $$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHCOCH}_{3}$$

question:
Addition of alcohols to aldehydes and ketones takes place in presence of dry $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ gas because it
(i) Protonates the oxygen of the carbonyl compounds
(ii) Increase the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon
(iii) Removes the excess moisture from the reaction
(iv) Helps the reaction to move in the forward direction
explanation: Dry hydrogen chloride protonates the oxygen of the carbonyl compounds and therefore, increases the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon facilitating the nucleophilic attack by the alcohol molecule. Dry $$\mathrm{HCl}$$ gas also absorbs the water produced in these reactions thereby shifting the equilibrium in the forward direction.

The decarboxylation of carboxylic acids involves the loss of carbon dioxide to form hydrocarbons.

explanation: Statement  A  is false because the decarboxylation of carboxylic acids actually involves heating their sodium salts, not the free acids, with soda lime (a mixture of sodium hydroxide and calcium oxide), not just calcium oxide. Statement B is true in the sense that calcium salts of carboxylic acids can undergo decarboxylation, although the common practice is to use the sodium salts with soda lime. Correct option is (3)

Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: Aromatic carboxylic acids do not undergo Friedel-Craft's acylation.

Reason: The carboxyl group in aromatic carboxylic acids is a meta-directing group that deactivates the ring towards electrophilic substitution reactions.
explanation: The assertion is true; aromatic carboxylic acids do not undergo Friedel-Craft's acylation because the strong deactivating effect of the carboxyl group and the fact that the Lewis acid catalyst (like $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ ) used in Friedel-Crafts reactions tends to react with the carboxyl group. The reason is true in the context of electrophilic substitution reactions, but it is not the correct explanation for the inability to undergo Friedel Craft's acylation. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of phenol is 4.19 and that of benzoic acid is 10 .

Reason: Phenoxide ion is stabilised by non - equivalent resonance structures whereas benzoate ion by two equivalent resonance structures.
The correct option among the following is
explanation: Assertion is false but Reason is true.  $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$  of phenol is 10 and that of benzoic acid is 4.19.  This is because in the resonating structures  of benzoate ion, there will be equivalent resonating structures, with negative sign at oxygen. However, in case of phenoxide ion, it has non-equivalent resonance structure as negative charge appears on less electronegative carbon which leads to less stability.

options.text: Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation for Assertion.

Assertion is false but Reason is true

question:
Among the compounds
(i) $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COOH}$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{COOH}$
(iii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$ and
(iv) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$
The correct order of acid strength is
explanation:
Acidic strength depends on the stability of conjugate base formed. More stable the conjugate base, stronger is the acid The order of stability of conjugate base formed will be $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}>\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}>$ $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{COO}^{\ominus}>\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}^{\ominus}$ i.e.,
i $>$ ii $>$ iii $>$ iv.
(Increase in % s - character increases stability of conjugate base)
$\therefore$ $\,$ Correct order of acidic strength will be - $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COOH}>\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{COOH}$$>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}>\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$

question:
Assertion: Formic acid reduces mercuric chloride to mercurous chloride on heating, while acetic acid does not.


Reason: Formic acid is stronger acid than acetic acid.
explanation:
Formic acid behaves as reducing agent as it is oxidised to an unstable acid i.e., carbonic acid, which decomposes into $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
$\mathrm{HCOOH}+2 \mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{Hg}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{HCOOH}+\mathrm{HgCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{HCl}+2 \mathrm{Hg}($ Black $)$
Acetic acid remains unaffected by mercuric chloride, as it does not show reducing properties. So the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCOOH}$ does not give $\mathrm{HVZ}$ reaction.


Reason: $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCOOH}$ does not have $\alpha$ hydrogen atom.
explanation:
The HVZ reaction is a method used to introduce a bromine atom at the $\alpha$-carbon position (the carbon adjacent to the carboxyl group) of carboxylic acids.
But $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CCOOH}$ cannot undergo this reaction because the $\alpha$-carbon in this compound does not contain a hydrogen atom. So the option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Both formic acid and oxalic acid decolourize $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ solution


Reason: Both are easily oxidised to
$\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$.
explanation: Formic acid $(\mathrm{HCOOH})$ and oxalic acid $\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$ decolorize potassium permanganate by acting as reducing agents in an acidic medium, causing the reduction of permanganate ions $\left(\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}\right)$to manganese ions $\left(\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\right)$ and resulting in decolorization. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Formic acid is a stronger acid than benzoic acid.


Reason: $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ of formic acid is lower than that of benzoic acid.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955115/preptracker/questions/q_29138_explanation_1764955115496.png  The acidity of carboxylic acids is influenced by substituent groups. Electron withdrawing groups increase acidity, whereas electron donating groups decreases acidic nature. Benzoic acid, with a phenyl group as an electron donating group, is less acidic. Formic acid, lacking such groups, is a stronger acid and has a lower $\mathrm{pKa}$ value. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Fluoroacetic acid is stronger acid than bromoacetic acid.


Reason: Acidity depends upon the electron withdrawing effects of the fluorine and bromine.
explanation: Fluorine is more electronegative than bromine. Also fluorine is smaller than bromine. These two factors make fluorine highly electron withdrawing (-I effect), which increases the acidic nature of fluoroacetic acid. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: ${\text{m}}$ -Chlorobenzoic acid is a stronger acid than p-chlorobenzoic acid.

Reason: In $\mathrm{m}$-chlorobenzoic acid both -I effect and $+\mathrm{R}$ effect of $\mathrm{Cl}$ operate but in $\mathrm{p-}$ chlorobenzoic acid only $+\mathrm{R}$ effect of $\mathrm{Cl}$ operates.
explanation: In p-chlorobenzoic acid, both the electronreleasing $+\mathrm{R}$ and electron withdrawing $-\mathrm{I}$ effects are in operation, but in m-chlorobenzoic acid, only the -I effect operates. Consequently, $\mathrm{m}$-chlorobenzoic acid is a stronger acid compared to p-chlorobenzoic acid. The -I effect predominates in $\mathrm{m}$-chlorobenzoic acid, enhancing its acidity. So the option (3) is correct.

Reason: Carboxyl group increases the electron density at the meta-position.
explanation: The carboxyulic acid group is an electron withdrawing group. It decreases the electron density from ortho and para position and does not increase the electron density from meta position. The decreasing effect is less at meta position. This directs the incoming group to the meta position. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Substituents affect the stability of the conjugate base and acidity of carboxylic acids.


Reason: Electron withdrawing groups decrease the acidity of carboxylic acids.
explanation: Electron withdrawing groups enhance carboxylic acid acidity by stabilizing the conjugate base through inductive and resonance effects, facilitating charge delocalization and strengthening the acid's proton donating ability. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Methanoic acid reduces Tollens' reagent and Fehling solution.


Reason: Methanoic acid occurs in a variety of biting and stinging plants and insects.
explanation:
Methanoic acid is a potent reducing agent because of the presence of the aldehyde group. It has the capability to reduce compounds like Tollen's reagent and Fehling's solution.
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{HCOOH} & +\underset{\substack{\text { Tollen's } \\\text { reagent }}}{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}} \xrightarrow{\text { warm }} \\\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} & +\underset{\substack{\text { silver } \\\text { mirror }}}{2 \mathrm{Ag}} \downarrow\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{HCOOH} & +\underset{\substack{\text { Fehling } \\\text { solution }}}{2 \mathrm{CuO}} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \\& \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\underset{\text { Red ppt }}{\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O} \downarrow}\end{aligned}$
So the option (2) is correct

question:
Assertion: Carboxylic acids are reduced to alkanes on reaction with $\mathrm{HI}$ in presence of red phosphorus.


Reason: Melting point of carboxylic acids shows a regular pattern.
explanation:
Carboxylic acids can be reduced to alkanes by reacting with $\mathrm{HI}$ in the presence of red phosphorus. Initially this forms an acyl iodide $(\mathrm{RCOCl})$ and subsequently undergoes further reduction to yield an alkane with the removal of carbonyl oxygen and the generation of iodide ion $\left(\mathrm{I}^{-}\right)$.
The overall reaction can be represented as:
$\mathrm{RCOCl}+\mathrm{HI} \rightarrow \mathrm{RH}+\mathrm{ICl}$
Melting point of carboxylic acids are not in a regular order. So the option (3) is correct.

Reason: Resonance gives extra stabilization to carboxylic acid.
explanation: Hydrogen atoms in the carboxylic group are typically more readily replaced in chemical reactions compared to the hydrogen atoms in the alcoholic group.   https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955111/preptracker/questions/q_29082_explanation_1764955110911.png  This is due to the carboxylate ions stability and good leaving group properties. explanation:
The melting point of pure anhydrous acetic acid is $17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Crystal structure below this temperature looks like ice ( glacial) . So, pure acetic acid is known as glacial acetic acid.
Formic acid is stronger than acetic acid https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955102/preptracker/questions/q_29067_explanation_1764955101589.png Presence of $\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group in acetate ion shows $+\mathrm{I}$ effect and destabilizes acetate ion. Assertion: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ but not $\mathrm{HCOOH}$ can be halogenated in presence of red $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$.


Reason: Both formic acid and $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$ are highly soluble in water.
explanation:
It is HVZ reaction, in which carboxylic acids are halogenated using red $\mathrm{P}$ and halogen, to replace $\alpha$ - hydrogen. Formic acid lacks an $\alpha$ - carbon and cannot be halogenated this way. Aliphatic carboxylic acids up to four carbon atoms are water miscible due to hydrogen bonding with water.
${\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{COOH}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ (ii) }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}]{{{\text{ (i) C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{/}}\,\,{\text{Red}}\,\,{\text{P}}}}{\text{ClC}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{COOH}}$
So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Carboxylic acids do not give characteristic reactions of carbonyl group.

Reason: Carboxylic acids exist as cyclic dimers in solid, liquid and even in vapour state.
explanation: Carboxylic acids, being resonance stabilized compounds, exhibit a conjugated structure with the carbonyl group $(-\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O})$ and hydroxyl group $(-\mathrm{OH})$. They have a resonance hybrid with distributed double bond character, distinguishing them from simple carbonyl compounds. Carboxylic acids exists as dimer even in vapour state upto some extent. So the option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Carboxylic acids are stabilised by resonance.

Reason: Chloroacetic acid is weaker than acetic acid.
explanation:
$\mathop {{\text{Cl}} - {\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{CHO}}}\limits_{{\text{Chloroacetic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{acid}}} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{CHO}}}\limits_{{\text{acetic}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{acid}}} $
Chloroacetic acid's enhanced dissociation compared to acetic acid is attributed to the -I effect of the chlorine atom. This withdraws electron density and promotes proton release. Whereas in acetic acid, the methyl group induces $\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{I}$ effect, and destabilizes the acetate ion, reducing its dissociation tendency. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Consider the following acids :
(i) $\mathrm{F}_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{COOH}$ (ii) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}$
(iii) $\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{COOH}$ (iv) $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$
Correct order of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values is
explanation:
Higher the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ value or smaller the $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}$ value, weaker is the acid.
Acidic strength $\alpha-$-I'groups and
$\alpha \dfrac{1}{+ \text { 'I'groups }}$.
' $-\mathrm{F}$ ' is strong ' $-\mathrm{I}$ ' group.
$-\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ group is electron withdrawing group.
$-\mathrm{CH}_{3},-\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5}$ are '+I' groups
So, order of acidic nature is (i) $>$ (iii) $>$ (ii) $>$ (iv).
Hence, correct order of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values is
(iv) $>$ (ii) $>$ (iii) $>$ (i).

question:
The following steps are given for the mechanism of esterification of carboxylic acids.
I. The protonated esters looses a proton to give the ester.
II. Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen activates the carbonyl group towards nucleophilic addition of the alcohol.
III. Proton transfer in the tetrahedral intermediate occurs.
Arrange the following steps in their correct sequence.
explanation: Protonation of the carboxyl oxygen activates the carbonyl group toward nucleophilic addition of the alcohol. Proton transfer in the tetrahedral intermediate converts the hydroxyl group into $$-\mathrm{OH}_{2}$$ group which being a better leaving group, is eliminated as neutral water molecule. The protonated ester so formed finally loses a proton to give the ester. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949087/preptracker/questions/q_28170_explanation_1764949086970.png

question:
I. $$\mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCOCl}+\mathrm{POCl}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl}$$
II. $3{\rm{RCOOH}} + {\rm{PC}}{{\rm{l}}_3} \to 3{\rm{RCOCl}} + {{\rm{H}}_3}{\rm{P}}{{\rm{O}}_3}$
III. $$\mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{SOCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCOCl}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}$$
Which of the above reactions is/are preferred for the synthesis of $$\mathrm{RCOCl}$$ ?
explanation:
$$\mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{SOCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCOCl}+\mathrm{POCl}_{3}+\mathrm{HCl}$$
$$3 \mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{PCl}_{5} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{RCOCl}+\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$$
$$\mathrm{RCOOH}+\mathrm{SOCl}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCOCl}+\mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{HCl}$$
Thionly chloride is preferred because the other two products are gaseous and escape from the reaction mixture making the purification of the products easier.

question: The strongest acid amongst the following compounds is
explanation:
${\rm{ - I}}$ effect exerting (electron withdrawing) groups increases the acidic strength of an acid by withdrawing electron density toward itself, thereby weakening $$\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{H}$$ bond; thus, the release of $$\mathrm{H}^{+}$$ion by an acid becomes easier.
However, +I effect exerting ( $$e^{-}$$releasing) groups decreases the acidic strength by donating electron density to O-atom. Further, -I effect decreases with distance. Thus, the acidic strength of the given acids would be https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958986/preptracker/questions/q_27220_explanation_1764958985806.jpg https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958986/preptracker/questions/q_27220_explanation_1764958986396.jpg Hence, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958988/preptracker/questions/q_27220_explanation_1764958987369.jpg is the most acidic and the strongest acid than other given compounds.

explanation: The assertion is correct as aromatic aldehydes can be synthesized from hydrocarbons through side-chain chlorination followed by hydrolysis, which converts the chlorinated side chain into an aldehyde group. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

Assertion: Nitriles can be selectively reduced to aldehydes using diisobutylaluminium hydride $({\text{DIBAL}} - {\text{H}})$.

Reason: ${\text{DIBAL}} - {\text{H}}$ is a strong reducing agent that converts nitriles to primary alcohols.
explanation: The assertion is true as  ${\text{DIBAL}} - {\text{H}}$  is used for the selective reduction of nitriles to aldehydes. Correct option is (3)

Assertion: Alkenes can be converted into aldehydes and ketones using ozonolysis.

question:
Sodium benzoate
$\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{{{\text{NaOH}} + {\text{CaO}}}}{\text{A}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ Anh}}{\text{. AlC}}{{\text{l}}_3}}]{{{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{Cl}}}}{\text{B}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{ (ii) }}{{\text{H}}_3}{{\text{O}}^ + }}]{{{\text{(i) Cr}}{{\text{O}}_2}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}/{\text{C}}{{\text{S}}_2}}}{\text{C}}$
In the reaction sequence, conversion of B to C is known as
explanation: Sodium benzoate is heated with mixture of sodium hydroxide  $(\mathrm{NaOH})$ and CaO to give benzene (A). A undergoes Friedel-Craft's alkylation reaction to give toluene (B).  B undergoes oxidation with chromyl chloride $\left(\mathrm{CrO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right)$ in $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$ to give benzaldehyde (C). This conversion of (B) to (C) is known as Etard's reaction.  https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952532/preptracker/questions/q_108774_explanation_1764952532489.png

question:
Assertion: Dimethylsulphide is commonly used for the reduction of an ozonide of an alkene to get the carbonyl compounds.


Reason: It reduces the ozonide giving water soluble dimethyl sulphoxide and excess of it evaporates.
explanation:
We can reduce ozonides, by $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ to yield carbonyl compounds and dimethyl sulphoxide. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955126/preptracker/questions/q_29146_explanation_1764955126076.png Ozonide
So the option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Aldehydes and ketones are functional isomers of each other.


Reason: A better yield of aldehydes can be prepared by the action of Grignard reagents on ethyl formate instead of ethyl orthoformate.
explanation: Grignard reagents react with ethyl orthoformate more efficiently than with ethyl formate, giving higher aldehyde yields due to the enhanced reactivity of the aldehydes generated from ethyl orthoformate in forming tertiary alcohols. So the option (3) is correct.

explanation: Oxo process is used for preparing aldehydes. At high temperature and pressure, an alkene reacts with $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ (water gas) in presence of cobalt carbonyl catalyst. So the option (3) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Formaldehyde cannot be prepared by Rosenmund's reduction.


Reason: Acid chlorides can be reduced into aldehydes with hydrogen in boiling xylene using palladium or platinum as a catalyst supported on barium sulphate. This is known as
Rosenmund's reaction
explanation:
Acyl chloride (acid chloride) is hydrogenated over the catalyst palladium on barium sulphate. This reaction is called Rosenmund reduction.
Formaldehyde $(\mathrm{HCHO})$ cannot be prepared by Rosenmund's reduction because formyl chloride $(\mathrm{HCOCl})$ is unstable at room temperature. So the option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:

Hydrolysis of nitriles yields carboxylic acids with one more carbon atom than the original nitrile.
Amides can be directly dehydrated to form nitriles.
explanation: Statement A  is true because the hydrolysis of nitriles involves the addition of a carboxyl group to the carbon atom of the nitrile, resulting in a carboxylic acid with an additional carbon atom. Statement B is false because amides can be dehydrated to form nitriles, but this process is not direct; it usually requires dehydrating agents or conditions that favor dehydration. Correct option is (2)

Esters can be hydrolyzed in basic conditions to form carboxylates.
Basic hydrolysis of esters is reversible and the ester can be easily recovered.
explanation: Statement A is true as esters undergo saponification in the presence of bases to yield carboxylates and alcohols. Statement B is false because basic hydrolysis of esters is not reversible; unlike acidic hydrolysis, once the ester is converted into a carboxylate, it cannot easily be converted back to the ester under the same conditions. IV https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952433/preptracker/questions/q_108714_explanation_1764952433312.png

C - Q; Benzene reacts with acetylchloride in presence of anhy. $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}$ to give alkyl phenyl ketone. This reaction is known as Friedel-Craft's acylation reaction. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958504/preptracker/questions/q_108773_explanation_1764958503914.png
D - P; Toluene reacts with $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{KOH}$ followed by acidic hydrolysis to give benzoic acid. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958505/preptracker/questions/q_108773_explanation_1764958504749.png

question: A compound with molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is converted by the action of acetyl chloride to a compound of molecular mass 190. The original compound $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$ has
explanation:
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_{3}$
${ = 4 \times 12 + 10 \times 1 + 3 \times 16 = 106\,{\rm{u}}}$
Acetylation introduces $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}$ - group by replacing H of $ - \,{\rm{OH}}$ group.
Molecular mass of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}=16+12 \times 2+3 \times 1$
$=43 \mathrm{u}$
No. of ${ - {\rm{OH}}}$ groups in the compounds
$=$ Molecular mass of ${{{\rm{C}}_{\rm{4}}}{{\rm{H}}_{{\rm{10}}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}}$ +
2 $ \times $ Molecular mass of $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CO}-190$
$=106+43 \times 2-190=2$

question:
Several simple chemical tests are given to distinguish between the pair of compounds.
Which of the following pairs are correct?
(I) Propanal and propanone ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ Silver mirror test
(II) Acetophenone and benzophenone ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ Iodoform test
(III)Ethanal and propanal ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ Fehling's test
(IV)Benzoic acid and ethylbenzoate ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ Sodium bicarbonate test
explanation:
Silver mirror test ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ To distinguish between ketone and aldehyde
Iodoform test ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ To distinguish compounds containing ${\mathrm{\alpha-}}$ methyl ketone.
Fehling's test ${\mathrm{\rightarrow}}$ To distinguish between aldehyde and ketone.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}}}$ is used for benzoic acids. as a test. It won't give brisk effervescence of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}}$ with ethyl benzoate.

question: An organic compound has an empirical formula $$\mathrm{C}_{5} \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}$$. This liquid gives a pale yellow precipitate on warming with iodine in alkaline KI solution. The resulting salt upon acidification gives an acid which easily undergoes decarboxylaction on mild heating. The structural formula of the organic liquid could be
explanation:
Given compound gives iodoform test, a characteristic of methyl ketones or secondary alcohol $$\left(-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)$$. It may be option (1) or (2).
Acid undergoes decarboxylation by forming carbanion as an intermediate. Allylic carbanion is more stable.
$$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{2} \mathrm{COOH}$$
$$\rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$$

question: Match the root words in common/IUPAC names in Column I with the correct carbonyl compound in Column II and choose the correct options from the codes given below.
explanation:
"Form" in common naming refers to the simplest aldehyde derived from methane, hence Methanal (Q).
"Acet" refers to the two-carbon aldehyde, Ethanal (R).
"Propion" refers to the three-carbon aldehyde, Propanal (S).
"Carbaldehyde" is the suffix used for aldehydes attached to a ring structure in IUPAC naming, which matches Benzaldehyde (P).
Correct option is (3)

options.text: A - P, 	B - R,	C - Q,	D - S

Correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: Lower members of aldehydes and ketones are miscible with water.


Reason: Solubility of carbonyl compounds increases with increase in molecular weight.
explanation: The solubility of carbonyl compounds in water relies on hydrogen bonding, facilitated by hydrogen bond donating groups. However, as molecular weight increases and alkyl chains lengthen, hydrophobic interactions dominate, diminishing solubility due to the resistance of non-polar alkyl chains to interact with polar water molecules. So the option (3) is correct.

The carbonyl group undergoes weak intermolecular dipole-dipole interactions between the opposite ends of the $=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ dipoles. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955065/preptracker/questions/q_28950_explanation_1764955064716.png  However, these dipole interactions are weaker than the intermolecular hydrogen bonding found in alcohols. Thus, aldehydes have lesser b.p than alcohols. So the option (1) is correct.

question:
Consider the following three resonance structures
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958511/preptracker/questions/q_108776_question_1764958510888.png
The correct order of their stabilities is
explanation: The uncharged resonating structure is more stable than charged ones. So, (II) is more stable than I and III. Out of I and III, I is more stable as negative charge is acquired by more electronegative atom i.e., oxygen while in (III) positive charge is more oxygen atom.  So, correct order of stability of resonance structures are :  ${\text{II}} > {\text{I}} > {\text{III}}.$

question:
Assertion: Formic acid is a reducing agent.

Reason: Formic acid is used as preservative for fruits.
explanation:
Formic acid is easily oxidized to yield carbon dioxide and water and as a result, it functions as a strong reducing agent in various chemical reactions. Oxidation of formic acid can be represented as:
$\mathrm{HCOOH} \xrightarrow{[\mathrm{O}]} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
Formic acid is used in low concentrations to prevent the growth of fungi and to extend the shelf life of products such as marinades, juices, pickles, smoked fish or vegetable and fruit purees. So the option (2) is correct.
